text: The words ‘into the desert’ have been inserted to make it clear that the Exodus is alluded to; they are not in the text. Nor is the name ‘Caleb’, but the grammar of the Latin version necessarily implies that one person was excepted from the general doom, cf. Num. 14.24 and elsewhere. The Greek text has ‘And if there is one stiff-necked person, it is a marvel if he escapes’.
‘All these have a heart, though it be a heart void of reason’; or perhaps, ‘and in all these matters, the (human) heart is powerless to reason’, which is the sense of the Greek text.
The sense of the Greek text is probably rather ‘the acts which win his approval’.

vv. 18-22: In the Latin version, this is apparently regarded as an answer to the notions mentioned in verses 16, 17; in the Greek text, it seems to be a continuation of them, the answer being delayed till verse 24.
That is, according to the Greek text, the forces of nature are bound to obey it; the Latin version represents it as a warning against human disobedience.
This is the meaning of the Greek text. The Latin version has ‘denounced before the face of it’, which yields no satisfactory sense.
